events.event_description: First U.S. Consul to Greece (Gregory A. Perdicaris) took up his post at Athens, establishing consular relations in the capital.

evidence.quote: On June 27, 1837, the United States appointed Gregory A. Perdicaris to be its first Consul to Greece. He took up his post at Athens on January 7, 1838.

events.event_description: Charles K. Tuckerman, the first U.S. Minister Resident in Athens, presented his credentials, establishing the American Legation in Greece.

evidence.quote: On March 11, 1868, the United States appointed Charles K. Tuckerman as its first Minister Resident in Athens. He presented his credentials on June 16, 1868.

events.event_description: The United States closed its Legation in Athens due to the German occupation of Greece.

evidence.quote: The United State closed its Legation in Athens on July 14, 1941, due to the German occupation of Greece.

events.event_description: Anthony J. Drexel Biddle, Jr. was appointed Envoy Extraordinary and Minister Plenipotentiary to Greece, serving near the Government of Greece in exile in England, re-establishing a legation-level presence.

evidence.quote: Anthony J. Drexel Biddle, Jr., who was appointed Envoy Extraordinary and Minister Plenipotentiary to Greece on November 13, 1941, served near the Government of Greece established in England.

events.event_description: The American Legation was elevated to Embassy status when Anthony J. Drexel Biddle, Jr. was promoted to Ambassador Extraordinary and Plenipotentiary to the Government of Greece in exile.

evidence.quote: The United States promoted Anthony J. Drexel Biddle, Jr., to be the new Ambassador Extraordinary and Plenipotentiary to the Government of Greece on September 29, 1942.
